name: Common grape (food medium)
basis: per 100 g fresh weight (ripe European/table grape, Vitis vinifera, e.g. Thompson Seedless)

components.name: water
components.source: USDA FDC 174683 (Grapes, red or green, European type, raw)

components.name: D-fructose
components.evidence: Fructose 8.13 g/100g; co-dominant fermentable sugar (fructose > glucose in ripe grape)

components.name: sucrose
components.evidence: Sucrose 0.15 g/100g; minor in Vitis vinifera (grape stores hexoses, not sucrose)

components.name: starch
components.evidence: Negligible at maturity; grape berry accumulates free hexoses, not starch

components.name: L-tartaric acid
components.source: Muñoz-Robredo et al. 2011 Chilean J Agric Res 71(3):452 (table grapes, tartaric 1.28-7.45 g/L); MDPI Hortic. 9:1173 (2023) review
components.evidence: Signature acid of Vitaceae; typical ~5 g/L must ≈ 0.5 g/100g. Tartaric+malic = up to 90% of berry acids. Range 0.13-0.75 g/100g across cultivars

components.name: L-malic acid
components.source: Frontiers Plant Sci 2024 (malic at maturity 1-5 g/L); Muñoz-Robredo et al. 2011 (malic 0.38-29.92 g/L unripe->ripe)
components.evidence: Second major acid; declines during ripening to ~1-5 g/L ≈ 0.1-0.4 g/100g, typical ~0.25 g/100g. Thompson Seedless has low tartaric/malic ratio ~1.19

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Muñoz-Robredo et al. 2011 (citric traces to 1.03 g/L)
components.evidence: Minor acid, traces up to ~0.3 g/L ≈ 0.03 g/100g typical

components.name: succinic acid
components.source: Kelebek & Selli 2011 (grape organic acid profiles include succinic/fumaric); Springer J Food Meas Charact 2022
components.evidence: Detected in grape berry juice as a minor acid; present but not reliably quantified for this generic entry

components.name: fumaric acid
components.source: Springer J Food Meas Charact 16 (2022) 01698-3 (organic acids of 20 V. vinifera cultivars)
components.evidence: Minor acid, present in trace amounts

components.name: L-arginine
components.source: USDA FDC 174683; PMC10706070 (Marselan berries: arginine + proline most abundant free AA)
components.evidence: Most abundant amino acid in grape (major free-N source for yeast/microbes)

components.name: L-proline
components.evidence: Proline abundant free AA in grape (largely non-assimilable by yeast under anaerobiosis, but a real pool)

components.name: linoleic acid (18:2 n-6)
components.evidence: PUFA 0.048 g/100g, dominant grape fatty acid (mostly seed-derived); total fat only 0.16 g/100g

components.name: alpha-linolenic acid (18:3 n-3)
components.evidence: Present in trace within the PUFA fraction of grape seed lipid

remove.name: Silver
remove.reason: non-nutritive trace/contaminant element, not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Cadmium
remove.reason: toxic heavy-metal contaminant, not a nutrient

remove.name: Lead
remove.reason: toxic heavy-metal contaminant, not a nutrient

remove.name: Mercury
remove.reason: toxic heavy-metal contaminant, not a nutrient

remove.name: Rubidium
remove.reason: non-nutritive trace element, not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: trace contaminant; not a defined growth nutrient of grape

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: cobalt trace; no measured functional pool in grape (no B12), FooDB noise

remove.name: Pentadecanoic acid
remove.reason: odd-chain (C15:0) fatty acid is a dairy/ruminant marker, implausible in grape; FooDB predicted/trace

remove.name: Gamolenic acid
remove.reason: gamma-linolenic acid (18:3 n-6) implausible/negligible in grape flesh; FooDB predicted

remove.name: Heptanoate
remove.reason: C7 fatty acid, trace volatile artifact, not a growth-relevant nutrient pool

remove.name: Hexanal C6H12O
remove.reason: volatile aroma aldehyde (trace), not a quantitative growth substrate

missing_added: L-tartaric acid (signature grape organic acid, absent as quantified value)
individual sugars glucose and fructose with real amounts (fructose > glucose)
keep_uncovered: Malvidin 3-(6-coumaroylglucoside) (anthocyanins, genuine grape phytochemicals, no BiGG met)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 174683 (Grapes, red or green, European type such as Thompson seedless, raw; SR Legacy)

Muñoz-Robredo et al. 2011, Chilean Journal of Agricultural Research 71(3):452-458 (sugars and organic acids in commercial table-grape varieties; tartaric 1.28-7.45 g/L, malic 0.38-29.92 g/L, citric traces-1.03 g/L)
Characterization of varietal effects on acidity and pH of grape berries, Frontiers Plant Sci 2024, PMC11499634 (malic at maturity 1-5 g/L)
Grape Tartaric Acid: Chemistry, Function, Metabolism, and Regulation, Horticulturae 2023, 9(11):1173 (tartaric+malic up to 90% of berry acids)
Phenolic contents, organic acids and antioxidant capacities of 20 grape cultivars, J Food Meas Charact 2022, doi:10.1007/s11694-022-01698-3 (succinic, fumaric detected)
Water status / GABA-pathway amino acids in Marselan berries, PMC10706070 (arginine + proline most abundant amino acids)
notes: Dominant carbon/energy sources for a microbe on grape are the two free hexoses fructose (~8 g/100g) > glucose (~7 g/100g), followed by the organic acids tartaric (~0.5 g/100g, the Vitaceae signature acid) and malic (~0.25 g/100g); citric/succinic/fumaric are minor. Arginine and proline dominate the amino-acid (nitrogen) pool. Organic-acid amounts are cultivar- and ripeness-dependent midpoints; ranges given in evidence/sources. Removed heavy-metal contaminants and FooDB-predicted/implausible lipids (odd-chain C15:0, GLA). Anthocyanins, flavan-3-ols, stilbenes (resveratrol), flavonols and plant sterols are genuine grape phytochemicals kept as uncovered (no BiGG metabolites).
